title: NOC 52119 – Other Technical and Coordinating Occupations in Motion Pictures, Broadcasting and the Performing Arts | GTR Immigration
description: The backbone of Canada’s world-renowned "Hollywood North" consists of the skilled coordinators and technicians under NOC 52119. From gaffers in Vancouver to costume supervisors in Toronto, these TEER 2 professionals are vital to the production lifecycle. For immigration, this category is highly favored in provinces with booming film industries, offering robust pathways for those with specialized technical expertise.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category5 – Occupations in art, culture, recreation and sportTEER Level2Major Group52 – Technical occupations in art, culture and sportSub-Major Group521 – Technical occupations in art and cultureMinor Group5211 – Technical occupations in motion pictures, broadcasting and the performing artsNOC Code52119

duties: Coordinate the work of camera operators and technical staff during broadcasts.
Supervise gaffers and stagehands who rig and dismantle lights and sets.
Design and coordinate complex special effects such as pyrotechnics or crashes.
Study scripts to determine necessary costumes and oversee acquisition and maintenance.
Apply professional and special effects make-up on performers.
employment_requirements: Completion of a college program in broadcasting, theatre arts, or a related field is typically required.
Several years of experience in a related support or assisting occupation are usually mandatory.
Portfolio of work is required for floor managers and make-up artists.
job_outlook: The outlook is Moderate to Favourable, specifically in B.C. and Ontario. As streaming services continue to produce original Canadian content, the need for technical coordinators remains resilient.
labour_market_demand: Canada’s film and television sector has seen record-breaking investment in recent years. Demand is particularly high for specialized roles like special effects coordinators and lighting technicians. With massive production hubs in British Columbia and Ontario, international workers often secure LMIA-exempt work permits through the Global Talent Stream or significant film-tax-credit-driven hires.
Global Hub: Work on major Hollywood productions and international streaming content.
Specialized Infrastructure: Access to state-of-the-art studios like Pinewood and North Shore.
Union Benefits: Many roles are protected by strong unions (IATSE, NABET), ensuring high wages and safety.
pathways: BC PNP Tech / Film Specializations: Fast-track options for experienced crew members.
OINP Employer Job Offer: For those with an offer from an Ontario-based production company.
Express Entry: General FSW or CEC draws for those with one year of Canadian experience.
